data.title: Apellis Pharmaceuticals: The Picture Becomes Clearer
data.description:
When I last looked at Apellis Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:APLS) in early March of this year, I concluded that article by stating the picture was a bit of a 'mixed bag'. However, the stock merited a small position via covered call orders while waiting for the picture to become clearer. Since then, the company has seen some positive progress on the pipeline front and bolstered its balance sheet. Apellis also reported quarterly results on Thursday. Taken together, this merited a revisit of this commercial-stage biopharma concern. An updated analysis follows below.

Apellis Pharmaceuticals' headquarters are located in Waltham, MA. The company's primary asset is a peptide-based inhibitor of the complement proteins C3 and C3b called pegcetacoplan, from which it derives three recurring revenue streams. Pegcetacoplan, since 2023, has been approved to treat geographic atrophy secondary to age-related macular degeneration and geographic atrophy or GA. It is the first approved treatment for GA and is known by its brand name SYFOVRE®, which is approved in the U.S. and Australia.

Its systemic version of pegcetacoplan is called Empaveli and garnered FDA approval in 2021 to treat a rare, life-threatening blood disease known as paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria or PNH. Apellis also receives collaboration and license fees, primarily from the sales of Empaveli outside of the U.S., where it is known by its brand name Aspaveli® and marketed via its partner Sobi. The stock currently trades just above $23.00 and sports an approximate market capitalization of $2.5 billion.

Recent Developments:

Wednesday Empaveli was approved for two additional indications for those 12 years old and over. They are C3 glomerulopathy and primary immune complex membranoproliferative glomerulonephritis. This followed some additional encouraging data from a Phase 3 study for these two indications that was disclosed in early June. These two indications affect some 5,000 Americans.

Approval makes Apellis eligible for an additional $25 million payment from a $300 million royalty purchase agreement with Sobi (SWOBF). This entitles Sobi to 90% of future sales of Aspaveli®, which again is Empaveli's brand name outside of the U.S. This agreement was signed early in July and Apellis has already received the other $275 million upfront from the arrangement. Prior to this agreement, Apellis was garnering tiered royalties on ex-U.S. sales of Aspaveli ranging from high teens to high twenties per a deal signed with Sobi in 2020. Sobi will now book 90% of ex-U.S. royalties until agreed to upon caps being achieved. Once this happens at some point in the future, 100% of all ex-U.S. royalties will revert back to Apellis.

Empaveli is also in mid-stage development for other indications. Two pivotal studies evaluating it to treat Delayed graft function or DGF and Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis or FSGS. The company is also working on a combination therapy utilizing SYFOVRE as an improved treatment for GA. A Phase 2 study along these lines was just initiated.

Second Quarter Results:

Apellis Pharmaceuticals posted its Q2 numbers on July 31st. The company delivered a GAAP loss of 33 cents a share, 15 cents a share better than expectations. The better-than-expected bottom line was driven by lower cost of goods ($13.6 million compared to $23.1 million in the same period a year ago). R&D expenses also dropped to $67 million from $78 million.

Revenues fell just over 10.5% on a year-over-year basis to $178.5 million, some $10 million below the consensus. SYFOVRE® sales rose six percent from the same period a year ago to $150.6 million. Apellis Pharmaceuticals booked $20.8 million of EMPAVELI U.S. net product revenue, as well as $7.1 million in licensing and other revenue associated with the Sobi collaboration.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst community is quite mixed about the current prospects of Apellis Pharmaceuticals. So far this week, eight analyst firms including Needham, Citigroup, and Stifel Nicolaus, have reissued Buy ratings. Two of these contain minor upward price target revisions. Price targets range from $29 to $60 a share, with a mean target of approximately $40. Four analyst firms including Goldman Sachs and Wells Fargo maintained Hold ratings. Price targets among these pessimists range from $21 to $29 a share.

Just over 20% of the shares outstanding are currently held short. Several insiders have been frequent and consistent sellers of the stock for quite some time. They have sold just over $4.3 million worth of equity collectively in July. Apellis ended Q2 with approximately $370 million worth of cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet. This does not include the recently upfront payment for royalties agreed to with Sobi. Management has stated it has funding in place to reach sustained profitability.

Conclusion:

Apellis Pharmaceuticals lost $1.58 a share on just over $781 million in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has losses dropping to $1.15 a share in FY2025 as sales rise to $858 million. They project red ink will fall to 79 cents a share on revenue growth in the high single digits in FY2026.

While Apellis remains unprofitable, the company appears to be on the right path. It now has funding in place to reach profitability in FY2027 (current consensus sees a $1.07 a share profit), with earnings rising steadily to a projected $3.70 per share. FDA approval this week for an additional two indications for Empaveli provided more clarity.

In short, the longer-term picture for Apellis has become clearer. I looked at this name early this year. Therefore, I will maintain my small holding in APLS within covered call positions. I am not chasing Thursday's 17% rally in the equity as a decent portion might have consisted of short covering, given that one of every five shares in the stock is held short. That said, if the stock moves back down to $20 in the next pullback in the overall market, I will probably add some exposure to APLS with additional covered call trades.

data.title: Columbia Is Cheaper And Growing Abroad, But Not Yet An Opportunity
data.description:
Columbia Sportswear Company (NASDAQ:COLM) posted a very positive quarter, which, like many other names in apparel wholesale, was at least in part benefited from early shipments to avoid tariffs. Still, even with this in consideration, the company is doing very well in its international markets (~40% of revenues).

The company's stock price is now 50% below where I have generally analyzed it and considered it a Hold in 2024. Although I recognize the company's quality, I believe the stock is coming down from an overvalued position, and although the new price is more fair, it is not an opportunity. I maintain a Hold rating but will follow Columbia with interest.

Turning around just to face tariffs

As commented in my Q1'25 article, Columbia was just starting to turn around the boat from the storm that was the outdoors negative cycle post-COVID, when the tariff clouds started to tint the sky again (readers will allow me some poetic license when talking about an outdoors company).

If we look back on recent years, revenues had not decreased much in 2022/23/24 as the outdoors cycle turned, but profits had, because the company had over-inventoried (along with the industry) and had to set up large clearance efforts. Margins deteriorated significantly.

In late 2024, early 2025, the company started to show signs of recovery; for example, the company had already decreased most of its clearance activity, and margins started to grow in Q4'24. In Q1'25, North American (60% of sales) revenues were only moderately down, with international up close to double digits (LAAP up 14%, EMEA 7%), and again, gross margins grew because part of the sales replaced were low-value clearance sales.

Now in Q2'25, the trend has continued. The NA market was still down (2%, especially in DTC, where most clearance happened), but International did extremely well, with EMEA up low twenties, China up mid-teens, and LAAP (Latin America and Asia) up 12% on aggregate. These are not small, insignificant segments, but rather 40% of revenues; they move the needle.

Further, the company is showing growth in global search trends (which, coming from Google, do not reflect strength in Asia and China correctly) and maintaining and even growing in the U.S.

Part of the NA market resiliency came from wholesale orders shipping earlier to save on tariff duties. This will revert in 2H25, with the company guiding Q3'25 LSD down. Still, Q3'24 was a quarter with a lot of clearance (US DTC was up MSD on clearance alone), meaning margins might improve a little despite aggregate falling sales.

Gross margins recovered even more, 120bps for the quarter. Again, by replacing empty-calorie sales from clearance, profits improve despite revenues being more stagnant in some markets.

With already three years into the outdoor market negative cycle, we could have seen a potential cyclical recovery. Alas, tariffs change the picture a lot.

More info on tariff impact

My main take on tariffs for any retailer or consumer product company is that it is hard to know what will happen because of the second and third-order effects. A company or product line may gain share or not, but the product category might suffer from income or substitution effect (people have less disposable income to go out because now other categories are more expensive). Not to mention the derivative effects of higher macro challenges on jobs and income, or the wealth effects of stock markets falling like they do on the first day of August. That is, we don't know what will happen, and that is the main conclusion. We need to remain cautious and conservative regarding the downside of estimates, in my opinion.

Within that context, Columbia provided information on the impact of tariffs. We already knew that direct exposure to China was low, and management said on the call that in FY26, there will be no imports from China. One point not clarified is how much the indirect exposure to Chinese-produced inputs, which will be taxed at the Chinese rates, even if incorporated in products manufactured in a third country. I would believe this exposure is greater in things like zippers, and especially in technical fabrics and coatings that require large chemical operations.

Further, 70% of imports to the U.S. for FY25 were already imported before August 1st, and based on a 10% reciprocal global tariff (already higher on August 1st), the company expected a total impact for the year of $35/40 million, mostly to be felt in 2H25. This is already higher today (most Asian countries' tariffs have increased to 20% or more). Further, the impact will be higher in FY26 as more imports will be tariffed. Columbia clarified that it imports $800 million FOB to the U.S. every year, implying an impact of about $80 million on 10% tariffs and $160 million at 20% tariffs.

Further, the company clarified that it will not raise prices on the Fall 2025 collection. This may help it gain market share and maintain shipments, but will obviously impact gross margins.

The stock is down 50% but is only fairish

Throughout 2024, Columbia hovered around $90 per share, and I consistently said that this price was not only not opportunistic, but high. The company was trading at 15/20x its cycle-average earnings.

That means, even without the shock coming from tariffs, the company's stock should have been lower to stir my attention. This ended up happening, and big time, but now the context has worsened significantly.

At a stock price of $49, the company has a market cap of about $2.7 billion. If we use the past 8 years as the representation of at least two cycles in the industry (without tariffs), the company generated an average operating income of $330 million, or $250 million after tariffs.

That is, the company continues to trade at more than 10x earnings even assuming that it can easily or rapidly return to cycle-average earnings, something that even in a more positive context could have taken one or two more years.

The majority of this profitability would have been converted into returns to shareholders, as the company dedicated most of its earnings to repurchases and dividends.

But to the already relatively positive valuation above, we have to add the lack of contemplation for the risks embedded in the tariffs. We do not know how gross margins will behave post-tariffs. If the company decides not to raise prices in FY26 and tariffs are 20%, then 50% of operating income ($160 million) will be lost. If it raises prices, it may lose market share, or the market might shrink because consumers have less disposable income.

When facing uncertainty, the market should offer a low price as compensation. That is, if we don't know how much below (but probably below) the cycle-average earnings of the past decade Columbia will generate in the next few years, then I want the stock price to reflect a value that is significantly below that average, not above.

For that reason, and even though Columbia has become much cheaper in the past six to seven months, I still believe the name is not attractive yet, and I will wait for lower prices or more certainty on future demand and profitability. Until then, I maintain a Hold rating on COLM.

data.title: This Is Not The Time To Give Up On Salesforce
data.description:
This is a deja vu moment for Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE:CRM). The company continues to post strong results but investors appear to be worried that the latest acquisition is a return to a less mature phase (as well as potentially fearing long-term threats from generative AI). I analyze that acquisition and come to the conclusion that it comes at a stark contrast to the past expensive deals and may create substantial shareholder value. CRM maintains a strong balance sheet even accounting for this acquisition alongside solid GAAP margins. I see a clear path to double-digit annual returns moving forward. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

CRM Stock Price

I last covered CRM in April, where I rated the stock a "Buy" because of the volatility in the software sector due to tariff uncertainty. The stock has not benefited from the broader recovery after giving up much of those gains following its earnings report.

I can understand investors if they're thinking that the company has gone back to its old expensive M&A ways, but at a great risk, I'm of the view that “this time is different.”

CRM Stock Key Metrics

CRM is one of the largest enterprise tech companies best known for its flagship customer relationship management product but now has a deep product portfolio ranging from employee communications to data analytics.

In the most recent quarter, the company delivered 8% YoY revenue growth to $9.8 billion, exceeding guidance of between $9.71 billion and $9.76 billion.

On the profitability side, the company delivered just 20 bps of non-GAAP operating margin expansion, perhaps due to tough comparables as the company had dramatically raised profit margins over the past few years.

The company maintained its mature capital allocation policy, returning essentially all non-GAAP profits to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases in the quarter.

The company ended the quarter with $17 billion of cash versus $8.4 billion of debt, but this net cash position is likely to change materially once the Informatica acquisition closes.

Looking ahead, management guided for the second quarter to see up to 9% YoY revenue growth to $10.2 billion.

For the full-year, management now expects up to 9% YoY revenue growth to be between $41 billion and $41.3 billion, up from the prior guidance of $40.5 billion to $40.9 billion.

On the conference call, management was upbeat regarding their prospects and offered some promising commentary. Management appeared to address the elephant in the room (decelerating top-line growth) as they noted that the last several years have seen many transformations to cash flow, share repurchase, profit margins, dividends and acquisitions. Management said that these transformations are “behind us” and that they were beginning a new “growth transformation” catalyzed by agentic AI. Management outlined expectations to hire “another 1,000 to 2,000 more salespeople” to improve their distribution and help accelerate top-line growth rates.

Is CRM Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

If one were just looking at the numbers, they’d think the stock would be making new all-time highs. The company continues to generate profitable growth and reward shareholders with share repurchases all while promising higher top-line growth ahead. Instead, the stock has struggled since the report. I suspect that this is due to the company announcing an $8 billion acquisition of Informatica Inc. (INFA) after having abandoned such talks in early 2024. Management has noted that this deal would be funded with “cash and debt,” which likely means the net cash balance sheet should be interpreted more as leverage-neutral.

Investors might be worried that this acquisition is the first of many in a return to the previous “growth at any cost” era. Long-time investors might have a bitter taste regarding the company’s history of M&A, as they have historically come at high valuations, perhaps the $28 billion valuation for Slack at 32x sales multiple is the most notable. This $8 billion acquisition, however, feels different. INFA is projected to generate $1.69 billion in revenue this year, placing the valuation at a 4.7x sales multiple, a notable discount to CRM’s own valuation. I expect CRM to be able to drive accelerated revenue growth at INFA due to being able to cross-sell across its larger distribution base. This acquisition makes a lot of sense to me even if I understand the investor hesitancy.

As of recent prices, CRM traded hands at just around 6x sales. Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain high-single-digit revenue growth for many years.

The stock was trading at just over 22x earnings and analysts are notably not expecting much operating leverage.

I expect the company to sustain at least 35% net margins over the long term. I base that assumption on the margins seen at Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Adobe Inc. (ADBE). That places the current valuation right around 18x long-term earnings. Given the net cash balance sheet, still-respectable top-line growth, and mature capital allocation policies, I see fair value as hovering in the 18x to 22x range as a reasonable estimate. With the stock still trading at the low end of that range, the stock looks priced for solid double-digit returns from the earnings yield and ongoing growth alone, even before accounting for multiple expansion potential.

CRM Stock Risks

It's possible that the INFA acquisition is just the first of many in a return to the old “grow at any cost” days. It's possible that top-line growth does not accelerate, as it should be acknowledged that this is already a $40 billion revenue run rate business. If management is unable to boost profit margins at an acceptable pace relative to decelerating top-line growth, then the stock might face pressure from the valuation multiple. Further, the enterprise software market could eventually become a more competitive field in which growth even turns negative. I note that the biggest near-term risk might be potential disruption from generative AI, something that I have touched upon in my recent downgrade of SentinelOne, Inc. (S). I'm becoming of the view that generative AI may eventually lead to mass layoffs, which would pressure seat-based pricing models such as CRM. Whereas I downgraded S due to the lack of profitability, CRM's solid profit margins are enough to keep me bullish despite those potential risks.

CRM Stock Conclusion

This looks like a clear buying opportunity for CRM. The company appears to be reaching an inflection point in growth rates after having focused on driving profitability in recent years. The latest acquisition looks highly accretive even before accounting for potential cross-selling synergies. Management has noted that this acquisition will not impact their mature capital return strategy, and I view it as just a matter of time before the stock re-rates higher. CRM shares many of the traits I look for in holdings of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Buy" rating for the stock.

data.title: Why Lumen Technologies Tumbled By Nearly 20% After Q2 2025 Results
data.description:
After Lumen Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:LUMN) posted second-quarter results, shares tumbled by nearly 20%. The firm posted a three-cent per share loss as revenue declined. Markets did not reward LUMN stock on its updated full-year 2025 financial outlook.

The disparity between a stronger full-year outlook and a stock decline sets up an opportunity for investors.

Second Quarter 2025 Results

Lumen reported a non-GAAP earnings per share of -$0.03. Revenue fell by 5.5% Y/Y to $3.09 billion. It achieved an outperformance on adjusted EBITDA of $725 million. When special items of severance, litigation, transaction and separation costs, and modernization and simplification costs are considered, its unlevered free cash flow rose from $54 million to $166 million. The figures are shown in the table below.

Lumen's Three Financial Milestones

Chief Executive Officer Kate E. Johnson views the company as a transformation story with three critical financial milestones. First, Lumen needs to have "a healthy balance sheet and free cash flow that supports its underlying transformation activities."

The company revised its outlook, where it expects a stronger FCF of $500 million.

Second, Lumen aims to return to EBITDA growth. It increased its run rate cost-out target to $350 million, up by $100 million. This is shown on the first item in the table above.

Lumen’s third milestone is returning to revenue growth. The CEO said that this comes from building the backbone of the AI economy. She said:

“We are pleased with the administration's AI action plan and recent tax legislation, which not only reduces regulatory barriers and helps accelerate our current network build-out, it also provides us with additional capital to invest in our nation's digital infrastructure.”

A favorable tax legislation, lower regulation, and capital investments for the nation are all tailwinds. Lumen continues to execute on its private connectivity fiber (“PCF”) contract worth $8.5 billion.

Risks

Lumen’s debt level is a manageable risk factor to monitor. Its long-term debt is $17.56 billion, of which $331 million is current maturities. The company strengthened its balance sheet through a $2 billion bond offering. It extended maturities from 2029 – 2030 to 2033. While the firm mentioned that the post-TSA exposure falls by over 60%, the $50 million saved from interest expenses is beneficial.

The firm refinanced its term loan in March, which cut annual interest expenses by around $100 million.

Opportunities

Lumen is prioritizing business in its “grow bucket” at 8.5%. Chief Financial Officer Christopher Stansbury recognized the importance of this revenue as having strong margins. This accounts for nearly half of its sales. This offsets the slowdown in VPN and Ethernet, where the business is falling in the double-digit percentage.

Harvest unexpectedly performed well, thanks to activity from the public sector. However, Lumen anticipates this segment to decline over time. Fortunately, the public sector exceeded expectations. It has the momentum to continue its outperformance.

The CFO believes that the company’s investments in the digital platform, physical network, and the emerging technology ecosystem expand its total addressable market. They will have meaningful growth and total company revenue in 2029.

Shareholders might speculate that LUMN stock is down today to price in the risk of pivoting from negative revenue toward growth in 2029. The firm needs to continue shifting its resources into growth areas. In effect, the stock is pricing in the risks of a slower pace of the transition. 119 ILA (in-line amplifier) sites as part of its private core fiber (“PCF”) contracts, IRU conduit deployments, and new route constructions are all long-term activities in this transition phase.

AI as a Pivotal Shift

Investors who read my coverage on Celestica Inc. (CLS) might appreciate that artificial intelligence is a powerful catalyst. AI forces customers to embrace a pivotal shift. Lumen expands the bandwidth that AI needs for real-time data processing. Senior Vice President and Investor Relations executive Jim Breen said on the conference call that AI is driving demand for “secure, uninterrupted access to critical business applications.”

Stock Grades

Lumen’s stock rating rose from a Hold to a Buy in May.

The stock grades improved for valuation, growth, and EPS revisions. It fell for profitability and momentum. The momentum score will worsen after Lumen shares fell by nearly 20% today.

Your Takeaway

Lumen is a long-term investment for the patient reader. The firm is transitioning the business to align with the first phase of a three-phase AI evolution. Investors may consider hyperscaler and data center providers instead. However, those who want exposure to the networking and connectivity market should consider Lumen Technologies.

data.title: Planes, Trains And Automobiles: Can They Handle The Impact Of Tariffs?
data.description:
Earnings season is giving investors a glimpse at how industrial stocks are handling the impact of U.S. tariffs. David Mau, VP, Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss his outlook for the sector.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: This earnings season has been a window into how some of the world's largest industrial companies are handling the impact of US tariffs. Joining us now to discuss his view on the sector, David Mau, VP, director, and portfolio manager with TD Asset Management.

David, great to have you back on the program.

David Mau: Nice to be here, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: We have no shortage of deadlines in this world, it feels like, when it comes to trade. We are having trade deals being signed. How has it impacted the industrial sector? What are we seeing here?

David Mau: I think, generally speaking, it's-- the industrial companies have had a bit of a difficult time. It's been challenging. Like you said, the uncertainty is really disrupting the normal flow of business. You see some companies where their customers are pulling forward orders. So they're trying to get ahead of the tariffs. And then you see some customers that are delaying their orders because they want more visibility on the tariffs. Right? So it's been fairly disruptive.

But again, I-- it does depend on which segment of industrials that we're talking about. If we look at some of the companies that are more exposed to, say, domestic reshoring or artificial intelligence, automation, data centers-- a lot of those stocks are actually doing quite well. Names like Deere (DE) or Caterpillar (CAT), Eaton (ETN), WSP (OTCPK:WSPOF)-- these guys are involved in construction, domestic activity, data center. Those stocks are trading at or near all-time highs. So it hasn't been that bad for them in terms of share price reaction.

But then we have other companies, like rails or airlines. Those guys are more levered to consumption trends and cross-border trade. Those guys have not been doing well at all. So, it really does depend on which segment of industrials-- or subsegment-- that we're talking about. But overall, I think these tariffs haven't been good for anyone. Just some companies have performed better than others.

Greg Bonnell: Let's start breaking them down then by those baskets. You said some are doing better than others in this environment. Let's start with the rails. What have you been hearing from those companies?

David Mau: Most of the rails, or pretty much all of the major Class I rails, have reported their second-quarter results over the last week or two. We start with the Canadian guys. CN Rail (CNI) reported somewhat of a lackluster result for Q2. I think revenues were down a tiny bit, or close to flat. Earnings were also flattish, I would say. The good news for CN is that they did improve their efficiency ratio. So they're making progress on profitability, which has been positive.

CP just came out yesterday. Their results were actually much better than CN. They had growth in the top line, they had growth in volumes, they had growth on earnings. I think some of that is a result of the merger that they did with Kansas City Southern a couple of years ago. So their network is, I would say, relatively stronger than what CN has right now. But at the end of the day, they're still being impacted by tariffs.

Turning to the US rails, UNP reported a fairly decent result in Q2. The other major Class I rails would be-- which would be CSX and Norfolk Southern (NSC)-- they had softer numbers. The outlook hasn't been great. But I would say all of this has been overshadowed by the recent announcement that UNP is going to take over Norfolk Southern. And that's a very big announcement. I think that's going to create a truly transnational rail network across the United States. And in fact, I think it's the biggest rail merger in the history of rails. So it's a big deal.

Greg Bonnell: I guess people always say it. When you can see M&A activity like that, it gets people interested in the space. That's the rails. What about the airline sector? How are they faring in this environment?

David Mau: I think the airline sector is fairly mixed. When we saw the air-- we'll talk about the US airlines first. The US airlines, when they reported over the last week or two, what we saw was that the airlines that have a heavy exposure or heavy reliance on the domestic US market have done worse. And that's simply because the domestic leisure travel segment in the US, those consumers are feeling the pinch of a tougher economy. So they haven't been traveling as much. And we're seeing the airlines start to cut capacity on those domestic US routes and even lower seat prices.

Now, on the other hand, the airlines that have more exposure to international travel-- so that would be Delta (DAL) and United (UAL)-- they have more exposure to international routes versus, say, someone like a Southwest (LUV) or an American Airlines (AAL). They're actually doing OK. They're seeing continued strong demand for international travel, continued strong demand for premium cabins-- so your premium economy, your business class, your routes to Europe. Those are all still growing pretty good. And so we're seeing airlines add capacity on those routes, and they're even able to maintain or improve pricing on those seats. Again, so it's a bit uneven. It depends on where exactly a particular airline has more exposure.

Greg Bonnell: I guess what kind of customer they're catering to. And if you're flying business or premium, then you got some money as well.

David Mau: Yeah. So the higher-end customer seems to be a lot more resilient right now than your basic economy travel customer. And that's been the trend that we've seen since the first quarter, even the fourth quarter last year. And that continues. I think the question is, how much longer does that premium segment traveler-- how much longer can they continue to spend if the economy continues to get worse?

Greg Bonnell: We've done rails. We've done planes. Let's get to the autos. We've seen deals, trade deals-- now the US and Japan, now South Korea. What does that mean for the auto sector?

David Mau: I think, speaking to the Japan and Korea trade agreements, it's quite good for those automakers-- so your Toyotas (TM), your Hyundais (OTCPK:HYMTF), your Kias, Hondas (HMC). And those share prices have gone up when those deals were announced. It's not, I think, that great for the American automakers. Because when you look at the big picture, if Japan-made cars are being tariffed at a 15% rate, whereas American-made cars, where those cars-- a lot of those cars, are made in Canada and Mexico-- are still being tariffed at a 25% rate with the possibility of that going up to 35%, the American automakers are going to be at a disadvantage.

And the other thing is, when the Japan trade deal was announced-- I guess it was last week or possibly this week-- one of the parts of the deal was that Japan would remove trade barriers for American cars to enter Japan. Well, we know that in the US, in America, there's definitely appetite for Japanese-made cars being imported to America.

Greg Bonnell: But does it go the other way?

David Mau: So the question is, even though trade barriers have been removed in Japan for American-made cars to be exported there, is there really an appetite in the Japan domestic market for a Ford (F) or a Chevy (GM) or an American OEM to really gain any kind of market share there? I think that remains to be seen.

Original Post
